name: Jamshid
description: Jamshid, also known as Yama in some traditions, is a prominent figure in Persian mythology, revered as the fourth king of the world in the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi. Born with divine right, he ushered in a golden age characterized by unprecedented prosperity, peace, and technological advancement. He is credited with dividing people into social classes: priests, warriors, farmers, and artisans, establishing a structured society. Jamshid also introduced the celebration of Nowruz, the Persian New Year, marking the vernal equinox. His reign was marked by incredible achievements, including the creation of weapons, weaving of clothes, extraction of gems from the earth, and construction of elaborate buildings. He even commanded demons to assist in his architectural endeavors. However, Jamshid's hubris grew with his power and success. He declared himself a god, abandoning Ahura Mazda and alienating his people. This act of arrogance, known as 'farr' (divine glory) leaving him, led to his downfall. The tyrannical Zahhak, empowered by Ahriman, seized the throne, plunging the world into darkness and oppression, ending Jamshid's legendary reign. Jamshid's story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of pride and the importance of humility, even for those blessed with extraordinary gifts and power.
subtitle: The Radiant King of Persia

keyMyths.title: The Golden Age
keyMyths.description: Jamshid's reign brought an era of peace, prosperity, and technological advancement to the world. He improved life for all his subjects and ushered in an age of discovery. This period ended with his arrogance.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Nowruz
keyMyths.description: Jamshid is credited with establishing Nowruz, the Persian New Year, celebrating the vernal equinox. This tradition continues to be a significant cultural event in Iran and other parts of the world. It symbolizes renewal and rebirth.

keyMyths.title: The Fall from Grace
keyMyths.description: Jamshid's hubris led to his downfall, as he declared himself a god and lost his divine glory. Zahhak then usurped the throne, ushering in an age of darkness. This illustrates the danger of unchecked power.

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Jamshid.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Jamshid.
